Upon arrival at the designated location at 0500 hours, the night shift supervisor was instructing the day supervisor regarding the operation of the software and the functionalities of the XFire system. The day supervisor possessed a basic understanding of the system's operation.
Run One
The first gun run was initiated, and a switch scan was performed at this time. Additional scans were made at tie-in and prior to pumpdown. The supervisor pulled into plug depth and scanned the switches to set the plug. A positive plug set was indicated. The supervisor commenced shooting the first zone, but he was unsure of the scan/firing procedure; I instructed the supervisor during the shooting phase. The first gun was shot stationary due to the short spacing from the plug to the first shot. Then, the 10-gun string ASF/POOH was shot.
During the plug set operation, my focus was on the software issue that we encountered the previous day*(Tier 1 OK). I was checking the two windows to confirm the plug set.
Note*: During the plug switch scan, the Company Man expressed dissatisfaction with the performance of the XFire system from his experience the previous day.
Run Two
Gun run two was RIH, and a switch scan was performed by the supervisor. Additional scans were made at tie-in and prior to pumpdown. The supervisor pulled into plug depth and scanned the switches to set the plug. A positive plug set was indicated. The first gun was shot stationary due to the short spacing from the plug to the first shot. During the firing sequence of gun two, the supervisor scanned the switches again. He fired gun two before completing the scan sequence, from the scan it appeared to fire. He Continued to shoot the remaining gun string; he called all eleven shots as fired off his shot sheet.
I reviewed the switch scans to confirm gun two fired; I visually inventoried the fired switches and found gun eleven was not fired. The gun was confirmed un- fired on the surface. The supervisor and I met with the Company Man to review the situation with the unfired gun. We did not delve into details on the cause of the failure at this time. The Company Man replied that "he can change out equipment."
Note: During run two, I noticed that the supervisor was rescanning switches after each shot. I assumed there was an issue with the auto-scan. I called Eric and discussed the issue, and he said it was a procedural error by the supervisor. This was an extra unneeded scan. I instructed the supervisor to eliminate the extra scan.




Run Three
Prior to the start of run three, I was informed that the Company Man requested Hunting switches be used on the remaining job. A hotshot was initiated, and switches were on location at ~1300 hours. Gun run two was RIH, and a switch scan was performed by the supervisor. Additional scans were made at tie-in and prior to pumpdown. The supervisor pulled into plug depth and scanned the switches to set the plug. A positive plug set was indicated. ASF/POOH.
A Tier 1 FSM arrived to help transition to the Titan system. I removed the XFire system and helped install the Hunting panel. I reviewed with the crew the potential issue of having two switch systems on location and the possibility of the wrong switch being installed. All XFire switches were isolated from the loading area. During testing of the switches with the Hunting panel, switches could not be read. I asked the FSM if he changed the BRT XFire switch to a Hunting switch. A Hunting switch was installed in the BRT. All switch communications evaluated good.

*GO Civitas – XC*
 *Update on DC Insert Breakage*
This is a follow-up on the earlier reports of DC Inserts breaking. I visited location to better understand what the crews were seeing and to observe both the installation and cleanup process. They mentioned that *eight inserts had broken within the past 36 hours*, all involving the same two DCs and the same two retainer nuts that have been in service for some time.
I grabbed a recently rebuilt DC tremoved its retainer nut and insert. The Retainer Nut in my opinion was heavily torqued. After disassembly, I found the insert had also cracked.

*Initial Measurements*

*Retainer Nut Measurements*
(_See the attached Print Screenshot_)
• A. Internal Depth: 4.014”
• B. Overall Length: 4.125”
• C. Top of Thread to Main Shoulder: 0.627”
• D. Main Body Length: 3.498”
*Insert Measurements (XBH500-203)*
*13 samples* were measured at the site, and *13 samples* at the GO Facility.

*On-Site Samples:*
• 2 brand new inserts measured at *4.597” and 4.598”*
• 1 previously shaved-down insert, likely from the earlier Gen 1 retainer nut issue
• 10 used or broken inserts measured between *4.587” and 4.592”* (after compression)
*GO Facility Samples:*
• 3 shaved-down inserts measured between *4.567”–4.573”*
• 10 new inserts measured consistently at *4.597”*
*Key Observations*
• All three retainer nuts (two on-site, one at the facility) showed signs of wear and potential over-torquing, specifically, flared outer shoulders and worn internal shoulders.
• Retainer nut main body lengths were slightly below spec:
    ◦ Facility sample: *3.495”*
    ◦ On-site: *3.497”–3.498”*
• Insert lengths were a bit over tolerance:
    ◦ Print spec: *4.575”  Tolerances are set at ± 0.015”*
    ◦ Several inserts measured around *4.597”*, putting them *0.007”–0.008” over* the upper limit of the Tolerance. .020-.023 over Print Diagram.
*Summary*
The combination of slightly oversized inserts and worn retainer nuts appears to the cause of these Inserts cracking

XConnect was called because the engineer was receiving a short after gun 8. He was able to set the plug and shoot gun 1 before the switch showed a short. The engineer was able to read the switch with the surface test box through the slip rings, so the surface panels were swapped out to make sure there was not a short at the panel. The issue persisted and the decision was made to skip gun 2 through 7 and shoot guns 8 through 11.

Once on surface the shot guns were removed and the tool string verified again with the verifier box. The switch stem for gun 7 was removed from the tool string and when tested with the verifier box independently it showed “yes” for a detonator which indicated a short.
While troubleshooting the gun system the SQM noticed a lot of moisture on the bulkhead of gun 7, on other switch stems in the trailer, as well as in the sub cart used when breaking down guns. The concern about excessive moisture was communicated with the crew and they understood saying they had been cleaning the switch stems before installing them on new guns.

The switch stem was returned to the XConnect facility and further evidence of moisture and corrosion was discovered. 
The moisture was able to travel into the switch molding and reach the switch board creating corrosion that eventually caused the stem to short out.
